questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme of fatty acid synthesis is:
questions.explanation: ACC (acetyl-CoA carboxylase): converts acetyl-CoA → malonyl-CoA (requires biotin, ATP). Rate-limiting step of FA synthesis. Activated by: insulin, citrate (allosteric). Inhibited by: palmitoyl-CoA (end-product), glucagon/epinephrine (phosphorylation by AMPK), AMP. Malonyl-CoA also inhibits CPT-I (prevents FA oxidation during synthesis). FA synthase then adds 2-carbon units from malonyl-CoA. NADPH from HMP shunt and malic enzyme is required. Reference: Harper's — FA Synthesis.

questions.questionText: β-oxidation and fatty acid synthesis appear to be reverse reactions but differ fundamentally. The key regulatory feature preventing a futile cycle between them is:
questions.optionA: They use the same enzymes in reverse
questions.optionB: MALONYL-CoA (product of ACC, first committed step of FA synthesis) directly INHIBITS CPT-I (carnitine shuttle entry for β-oxidation) — ensuring that when synthesis is active, oxidation is blocked simultaneously in the same cell
questions.optionC: They occur in the same compartment
questions.explanation: β-oxidation vs FA synthesis — reciprocal regulation: KEY: malonyl-CoA. Fed state (insulin high): ACC ACTIVE → malonyl-CoA HIGH → (1) feeds FA synthesis (cytoplasm), (2) INHIBITS CPT-I → long-chain acyl-CoA cannot enter mitochondria → β-oxidation BLOCKED. Fasted state (glucagon/epinephrine): AMPK phosphorylates ACC → ACC INACTIVE → malonyl-CoA LOW → (1) FA synthesis stopped, (2) CPT-I RELIEVED → fatty acids enter mitochondria → β-oxidation ON. Other differences: synthesis (cytoplasm, NADPH, acyl carrier protein, palmitate only), oxidation (mitochondria, NAD+/FAD, CoA, any chain length). This malonyl-CoA switch is one of the most elegant regulatory mechanisms in metabolism and is a very frequently tested concept. Reference: Harper's — Malonyl-CoA Regulation.

questions.questionText: Ketone body synthesis (ketogenesis) occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Ketogenesis: exclusively in liver mitochondria. Pathway: acetyl-CoA → acetoacetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA (by HMG-CoA synthase, rate-limiting) → acetoacetate (by HMG-CoA lyase) → β-hydroxybutyrate (by β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase) or → acetone (spontaneous decarboxylation). Liver CANNOT utilize ketone bodies (lacks thiophorase/succinyl-CoA transferase). Brain, heart, muscle use ketone bodies as fuel. Increased in: starvation, uncontrolled diabetes, alcoholism. Reference: Harper's — Ketogenesis.

questions.questionText: Diabetic ketoacidosis results from excessive:
questions.optionC: Lipolysis → fatty acid oxidation → ketone body overproduction (due to insulin deficiency + glucagon excess)
questions.explanation: DKA: insulin deficiency (Type 1 DM) + glucagon excess → uncontrolled lipolysis → excess FFA to liver → accelerated β-oxidation → excess acetyl-CoA → overwhelms TCA cycle → ketogenesis → ketone body accumulation → metabolic acidosis (increased anion gap). Features: hyperglycemia, ketonuria, ketonemia, dehydration, Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath (acetone). Treatment: insulin, fluids, electrolyte replacement. Reference: Harper's — DKA.

questions.questionText: The citrate shuttle is used to transport:
questions.optionB: Acetyl-CoA from mitochondria to cytoplasm (for fatty acid synthesis)
questions.explanation: Citrate shuttle: acetyl-CoA (produced in mitochondria from pyruvate) cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane. Acetyl-CoA + OAA → citrate (citrate synthase) → citrate transported out via tricarboxylate carrier → in cytoplasm: citrate → acetyl-CoA + OAA (by ATP-citrate lyase/citrate cleavage enzyme). Acetyl-CoA used for FA synthesis. OAA → malate (malate dehydrogenase) → pyruvate (malic enzyme, generates NADPH) → returns to mitochondria. Reference: Harper's — Citrate Shuttle.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol synthesis is inhibited by:
questions.optionC: Statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors), dietary cholesterol, and bile acids
questions.explanation: Cholesterol synthesis regulation: HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting enzyme). Inhibited by: statins (competitive inhibitors — lovastatin, atorvastatin), cholesterol itself (feedback — SREBP pathway), bile acids, fasting/glucagon (phosphorylation by AMPK). Activated by: insulin, thyroid hormones (increase LDL receptors + synthesis). SREBP pathway: low cholesterol → SCAP escorts SREBP from ER to Golgi → proteolytic activation → enters nucleus → activates HMG-CoA reductase gene. Reference: Harper's — Cholesterol Regulation.

questions.questionText: Lipoprotein lipase (LPL) is activated by:
questions.explanation: LPL (lipoprotein lipase): located on capillary endothelium (attached via heparan sulfate). Hydrolyzes TG in chylomicrons and VLDL → releases FFAs for tissue uptake. ApoC-II is the essential cofactor/activator. ApoC-II deficiency (Type I hyperlipoproteinemia): cannot clear TG → severe hypertriglyceridemia, pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis. ApoB-48: chylomicrons. ApoB-100: VLDL/LDL (LDL receptor ligand). ApoE: chylomicron remnant/IDL clearance. Reference: Harper's — LPL.

questions.questionText: Zellweger syndrome is a peroxisomal disorder characterized by:
questions.optionD: Absence/deficiency of peroxisomes → inability to oxidize VLCFA, synthesize plasmalogens
questions.explanation: Zellweger syndrome (cerebrohepatorenal syndrome): peroxisome biogenesis disorder (PBD) — absent or severely reduced functional peroxisomes. Peroxisomes are essential for: VLCFA β-oxidation, plasmalogen synthesis, bile acid synthesis, phytanic acid α-oxidation. Results in: accumulation of VLCFA, phytanic acid; deficiency of plasmalogens. Features: severe hypotonia, seizures, hepatomegaly, renal cysts, craniofacial dysmorphism. Fatal in infancy. X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (X-ALD): VLCFA accumulation (ABCD1 gene). Reference: Harper's — Peroxisomal Disorders.

questions.questionText: Fatty liver (hepatic steatosis) occurs when:
questions.optionC: TAG synthesis exceeds VLDL secretion (imbalance in fatty acid input/output in liver)
questions.explanation: Fatty liver: TAG accumulates in hepatocytes when input (FA synthesis, FA uptake from blood) exceeds output (FA oxidation, VLDL secretion). Causes: alcohol (increased NADH/NAD+ shifts metabolism, inhibits FA oxidation), obesity, diabetes, starvation (increased FFA mobilization), protein/choline deficiency (impaired VLDL synthesis — no apoB), hepatotoxins (CCl4, phosphorus), drugs (methotrexate, tetracycline). NAFLD/NASH spectrum. Reference: Harper's — Fatty Liver.

questions.questionText: Refsum disease is caused by impaired α-oxidation of:
questions.optionC: Phytanic acid (due to phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase deficiency)
questions.explanation: Refsum disease: phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase deficiency → cannot α-oxidize phytanic acid (C20 branched-chain FA from dairy, meat). α-oxidation removes one carbon (converts to pristanic acid which can undergo β-oxidation). Phytanic acid accumulates → retinitis pigmentosa, peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar ataxia, elevated CSF protein. Treatment: dietary restriction of phytanic acid sources (dairy, ruminant fats). α-Oxidation occurs in peroxisomes. Reference: Harper's — Refsum Disease.

questions.questionText: LCAT (lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase) is important for:
questions.optionC: Esterification of cholesterol on HDL (maturation of HDL)
questions.explanation: LCAT: catalyzes esterification of free cholesterol → cholesterol ester (transfers fatty acid from lecithin/phosphatidylcholine to cholesterol). Acts on HDL particles. Activated by ApoA-I (HDL surface). Important for HDL maturation (nascent discoidal HDL → spherical mature HDL). CETP (cholesterol ester transfer protein) transfers cholesterol esters from HDL to VLDL/LDL. LCAT deficiency: fish eye disease/familial LCAT deficiency → corneal opacities, low HDL. Reference: Harper's — LCAT.

questions.questionText: Fish oil supplementation (omega-3 fatty acids) at high doses (3-4g/day) significantly lowers plasma triglycerides. The mechanism involves:
questions.optionB: EPA/DHA activate PPARα (like fibrates) → increase LPL expression and β-oxidation; simultaneously SUPPRESS SREBP-1c → decrease hepatic de novo lipogenesis and VLDL assembly/secretion → lower plasma TG by 25-45%
questions.explanation: Omega-3 TG-lowering — molecular mechanism: EPA (20:5 ω-3) and DHA (22:6 ω-3): (1) PPARα ACTIVATION: (a) Increase fatty acid β-oxidation genes (like fibrates), (b) Increase LPL expression → enhanced TG clearance from lipoproteins, (c) Decrease ApoC-III (LPL inhibitor) → further enhances LPL activity. (2) SREBP-1c SUPPRESSION: decrease hepatic de novo LIPOGENESIS → less TG synthesis. (3) Decrease VLDL ASSEMBLY: (a) Less TG substrate available, (b) Promote ApoB degradation → fewer VLDL particles secreted. (4) Anti-inflammatory: EPA competes with AA for COX/LOX → less pro-inflammatory PG/LT; produce RESOLVINS and PROTECTINS (pro-resolution mediators). Icosapent ethyl (Vascepa): pure EPA → REDUCE-IT trial → 25% CV event reduction. Reference: Harper's — Omega-3 Mechanisms.

questions.questionText: The hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) in adipose tissue is activated by:
questions.optionA: Epinephrine and glucagon (via cAMP → PKA phosphorylation)
questions.explanation: HSL: rate-limiting enzyme of lipolysis in adipose tissue. TG → DAG → MAG → glycerol + 3 FFAs. Activated by: epinephrine/norepinephrine (β-adrenergic → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylation of HSL + perilipin), glucagon. Inhibited by: insulin (activates phosphodiesterase → degrades cAMP, also activates protein phosphatase → dephosphorylates HSL). Perilipin must be phosphorylated for HSL to access lipid droplet. FFAs released to blood → albumin transport. Reference: Harper's — Lipolysis.

questions.questionText: Medium-chain fatty acid oxidation does NOT require:
questions.explanation: Medium-chain fatty acids (C6-C12): can enter the mitochondrial matrix directly WITHOUT the carnitine shuttle (carnitine-independent). They are activated to their CoA derivatives inside the matrix. This is why MCT (medium-chain triglyceride) oil is used in: carnitine deficiency, CPT deficiency, malabsorption syndromes. MCAD (medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase) deficiency: most common FA oxidation defect → hypoketotic hypoglycemia, dicarboxylic aciduria. Reference: Harper's — MCFA Oxidation.

questions.questionText: Bile acids are synthesized from cholesterol in the liver. The rate-limiting enzyme is:
questions.explanation: Bile acid synthesis: cholesterol → 7α-hydroxycholesterol (by cholesterol 7α-hydroxylase/CYP7A1, rate-limiting, regulated) → primary bile acids (cholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid). Conjugated with glycine or taurine → bile salts. Secondary bile acids (by colonic bacteria): deoxycholic acid and lithocholic acid. Bile acids are feedback inhibitors of CYP7A1 (via FXR nuclear receptor). Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine) interrupt enterohepatic circulation → upregulate CYP7A1 → lower cholesterol. Reference: Harper's — Bile Acid Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Deficiency of lipoprotein lipase or ApoC-II classically causes marked elevation of:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. LPL and ApoC-II are essential for hydrolysis of triglycerides in chylomicrons/VLDL. Defects produce massive TG elevation and pancreatitis risk.

questions.questionText: Peroxisomal beta-oxidation is especially important for:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Peroxisomes shorten VLCFA before further mitochondrial oxidation. Defects (e.g., Zellweger spectrum) cause VLCFA accumulation and neurologic dysfunction.

questions.questionText: CPT-I is physiologically inhibited by:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I, preventing simultaneous fatty acid synthesis and oxidation in fed state.

questions.questionText: Abetalipoproteinemia (Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionC: Microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) → inability to form chylomicrons and VLDL
questions.explanation: Abetalipoproteinemia: autosomal recessive, MTP deficiency → cannot assemble/secrete ApoB-containing lipoproteins (chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL — all absent in plasma). Features: fat malabsorption (steatorrhea from birth), failure to thrive, acanthocytosis (spiky RBCs — due to altered membrane lipid composition), retinitis pigmentosa, progressive ataxic neuropathy. All due to deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Treatment: massive vitamin E supplementation (delays neurological damage). Reference: Harper's — Abetalipoproteinemia.

questions.questionText: Tangier disease is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionD: ABCA1 transporter (ATP-binding cassette transporter A1 → impaired cholesterol efflux to HDL)
questions.explanation: Tangier disease: autosomal recessive, ABCA1 transporter deficiency → macrophages cannot export cholesterol to ApoA-I → HDL virtually absent, cholesterol esters accumulate in reticuloendothelial tissues. Features: orange tonsils (pathognomonic — cholesterol-laden macrophages), hepatosplenomegaly, peripheral neuropathy, premature coronary disease. Heterozygotes: HDL ≈ 50% of normal. ABCA1 is critical for the first step of reverse cholesterol transport (cholesterol efflux from peripheral cells to nascent HDL). Reference: Harper's — Tangier Disease.

questions.questionText: ApoE is important for the hepatic uptake of:
questions.optionC: Chylomicron remnants and IDL (via LDL receptor-related protein/LRP and LDL receptor)
questions.explanation: ApoE: recognition signal for hepatic receptors (LDL receptor and LRP). Present on: chylomicron remnants, VLDL, IDL, HDL. ApoE mediates hepatic clearance of remnant particles. ApoE genotypes: E2/E2 → defective binding to receptors → Type III hyperlipoproteinemia (familial dysbetalipoproteinemia — broad beta band on electrophoresis, palmar xanthomas). E4 allele: risk factor for Alzheimer's disease and cardiovascular disease. ApoE3: most common, normal function. Reference: Harper's — ApoE Function.

questions.questionText: Odd-chain fatty acid oxidation produces propionyl-CoA, which is converted to succinyl-CoA via:
questions.explanation: Odd-chain FA β-oxidation: final cleavage yields propionyl-CoA (3C) instead of acetyl-CoA. Propionyl-CoA → D-methylmalonyl-CoA (propionyl-CoA carboxylase, BIOTIN cofactor) → L-methylmalonyl-CoA (racemase) → succinyl-CoA (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, vitamin B12/adenosylcobalamin). Succinyl-CoA enters TCA cycle → this makes odd-chain FA glucogenic (unlike even-chain FA → only ketogenic). Vitamin B12 deficiency: methylmalonic aciduria (elevated MMA — more specific for B12 deficiency than megaloblastic anemia). Propionic acidemia: propionyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency. Reference: Harper's — Odd-Chain FA Oxidation.

questions.questionText: MCAD (medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase) deficiency presents with:
questions.optionA: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia during fasting (inability to oxidize medium-chain fatty acids)
questions.explanation: MCAD deficiency: most common inherited defect of fatty acid oxidation. Autosomal recessive. Cannot oxidize C6-C12 fatty acids adequately. Presentation: typically asymptomatic until fasting or illness → hypoketotic hypoglycemia (fatty acid oxidation impaired → ketogenesis reduced, liver cannot produce glucose via gluconeogenesis adequately without fatty acid-derived energy), vomiting, lethargy, seizures, sudden death (can mimic SIDS). Octanoylcarnitine (C8) elevated on acylcarnitine profile/newborn screening. A985G mutation in ACADM gene (most common). Reference: Harper's — MCAD Deficiency.

questions.questionText: A patient asks why statins are taken at bedtime. The pharmacological rationale based on cholesterol synthesis biochemistry is:
questions.optionA: Hepatic cholesterol synthesis (HMG-CoA reductase activity) peaks during FASTING/OVERNIGHT hours (diurnal rhythm, highest HMG-CoA reductase expression at ~midnight); taking short-acting statins at bedtime maximizes inhibition during peak synthesis
questions.optionB: Cholesterol is only absorbed at night
questions.optionC: Statins are better absorbed at night
questions.optionD: Statins cause less side effects at night
questions.explanation: Statin timing — chronopharmacology: HMG-CoA reductase expression follows a CIRCADIAN RHYTHM: peaks at midnight (during fasting/sleep → body synthesizes cholesterol when dietary supply is absent). Short-acting statins (simvastatin t½ ~2h, lovastatin t½ ~3h, fluvastatin t½ ~3h): taken at BEDTIME to ensure peak drug levels coincide with peak enzyme activity. Long-acting statins (atorvastatin t½ ~14h, rosuvastatin t½ ~19h): effective at ANY TIME (maintain inhibitory levels throughout 24h). Studies: simvastatin evening dosing → 20-30% greater LDL reduction than morning dosing. Atorvastatin: no significant difference (long t½). Other statin timing considerations: avoid with grapefruit juice (CYP3A4 inhibition, especially simvastatin/lovastatin/atorvastatin). Reference: Harper's — Statin Chronopharmacology.
questions.conceptTag: Statin bedtime HMG-CoA reductase circadian peak

questions.questionText: Reverse cholesterol transport refers to:
questions.optionB: Synthesis of cholesterol in the brain
questions.optionC: Transport of cholesterol from liver to peripheral tissues
questions.optionD: Transport of cholesterol from peripheral tissues back to the liver via HDL for excretion
questions.explanation: Reverse cholesterol transport (RCT): the pathway by which excess cholesterol is removed from peripheral tissues and returned to liver. Steps: (1) ABCA1 transporter exports cholesterol from cells to nascent/discoidal HDL (ApoA-I). (2) LCAT esterifies cholesterol on HDL surface → mature spherical HDL. (3) HDL delivers cholesterol to liver via: (a) SR-B1 receptor (selective uptake of CE), (b) indirect pathway via CETP transfer to VLDL/LDL → LDL receptor uptake. RCT is the main anti-atherogenic function of HDL. Reference: Harper's — Reverse Cholesterol Transport.

questions.questionText: Omega (ω) oxidation of fatty acids occurs in the:
questions.optionD: Endoplasmic reticulum (ER — microsomal, by CYP450 enzymes, oxidizes the methyl terminal/omega carbon)
questions.explanation: Omega oxidation: minor pathway, occurs in ER. CYP450 enzymes (CYP4A family) hydroxylate the terminal methyl (omega) carbon → successively oxidized to carboxylic acid → dicarboxylic acid formed. Then undergoes β-oxidation from either end. Normally a minor pathway but becomes significant when β-oxidation is impaired (e.g., MCAD deficiency, CPT deficiency) → dicarboxylic acids appear in urine (dicarboxylic aciduria — diagnostic clue). α-Oxidation: peroxisomal, for branched-chain FA (phytanic acid) — defective in Refsum disease. Reference: Harper's — Omega Oxidation.

questions.questionText: Familial hypercholesterolemia (Type IIa) is caused by mutations in:
questions.optionB: LDL receptor (defective/absent LDL receptors → markedly elevated LDL-cholesterol)
questions.explanation: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH): autosomal dominant (most common monogenic cause of premature CAD). LDL receptor mutations → impaired clearance of LDL from blood → very high LDL-C. Heterozygous (1 in 500): LDL-C 300-500 mg/dL, tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus, premature MI (age 40-50). Homozygous (1 in million): LDL-C 700-1200 mg/dL, xanthomas in childhood, MI before age 20. Treatment: high-intensity statins + ezetimibe + PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab). PCSK9 degrades LDL receptors → PCSK9 inhibitors increase LDL receptor numbers. Reference: Harper's — Familial Hypercholesterolemia.

questions.questionText: Jamaican vomiting sickness is caused by:
questions.optionB: Hypoglycin A (from unripe ackee fruit) — inhibits β-oxidation by inactivating acyl-CoA dehydrogenases
questions.explanation: Jamaican vomiting sickness: caused by hypoglycin A from unripe ackee fruit (Blighia sapida). Hypoglycin A is metabolized to methylenecyclopropylacetyl-CoA (MCPA-CoA) → irreversibly inhibits short-chain and medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenases and also CPT-I → impairs β-oxidation and gluconeogenesis → hypoketotic hypoglycemia (similar to MCAD deficiency presentation), severe vomiting, encephalopathy, fatty liver. Can be fatal. Dicarboxylic aciduria seen (compensation via omega oxidation). Reference: Harper's — Hypoglycin Toxicity.

questions.questionText: Peroxisomal β-oxidation differs from mitochondrial β-oxidation in that it:
questions.optionA: Is the only site of fatty acid oxidation
questions.optionB: Uses the same enzyme system and produces NADH for ATP
questions.optionC: Uses acyl-CoA oxidase (produces H2O2 instead of FADH2) and shortens very long chain fatty acids
questions.explanation: Peroxisomal β-oxidation: first step uses acyl-CoA oxidase → generates H2O2 (detoxified by catalase) instead of FADH2 → energy LOST as heat (not captured as ATP). Substrates: very long chain fatty acids (VLCFA, C>22), branched-chain FA, bile acid intermediates, prostaglandins. Peroxisomes shorten VLCFA → products (medium-chain acyl-CoA, acetyl-CoA) transferred to mitochondria for complete oxidation. Zellweger syndrome: peroxisomal biogenesis disorder → absent/non-functional peroxisomes → VLCFA accumulate. X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy: ABCD1 transporter defect → VLCFA accumulation. Reference: Harper's — Peroxisomal β-Oxidation.

questions.questionText: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) catalyzes the rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis, which is:
questions.optionA: Acetyl-CoA + CO2 → malonyl-CoA (requires biotin and ATP)
questions.explanation: ACC: rate-limiting enzyme of de novo lipogenesis. Acetyl-CoA + CO2 + ATP → malonyl-CoA + ADP + Pi. Requires biotin. ACC is activated by: citrate (allosteric activator — signals fed state), insulin (stimulates dephosphorylation → active). Inhibited by: palmitoyl-CoA (product feedback), phosphorylation by AMPK (fasting/exercise), glucagon/epinephrine (via cAMP → PKA → AMPK). Malonyl-CoA is also an inhibitor of CPT-I → when synthesis is on, oxidation is off (reciprocal regulation). Reference: Harper's — ACC Regulation.

questions.questionText: Wolman disease is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionD: Lysosomal acid lipase (LAL) → cholesterol esters and triglycerides accumulate in lysosomes
questions.explanation: Wolman disease: severe infantile lysosomal acid lipase (LAL/LIPA) deficiency → cholesterol esters and triglycerides cannot be hydrolyzed in lysosomes → massive accumulation in liver, spleen, adrenals, intestine. Features: hepatosplenomegaly, steatorrhea, failure to thrive, bilateral adrenal calcification (pathognomonic on imaging), death by 6 months. Cholesterol ester storage disease (CESD) is the milder form (partial LAL deficiency — adults: hepatomegaly, dyslipidemia, accelerated atherosclerosis). Treatment: sebelipase alfa (enzyme replacement). Reference: Harper's — Wolman Disease.

questions.questionText: Ketone body synthesis (ketogenesis) occurs in the:
questions.optionD: Liver mitochondria (ketone bodies cannot be utilized by liver)
questions.explanation: Ketogenesis: exclusively in liver mitochondria (hepatocytes). Key enzyme: HMG-CoA synthase (mitochondrial, rate-limiting for ketogenesis — different from cytoplasmic HMG-CoA synthase for cholesterol). Pathway: Acetyl-CoA → acetoacetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA → acetoacetate (+ acetyl-CoA). Acetoacetate → β-hydroxybutyrate (by β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase, NADH) or → acetone (spontaneous decarboxylation). Liver CANNOT use ketone bodies (lacks succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase/thiophorase). Brain, heart, muscle, kidney can use them. Reference: Harper's — Ketogenesis.

questions.questionText: The lipoprotein with the highest triglyceride content is:
questions.explanation: Lipoprotein composition comparison: Chylomicrons: highest TG content (~85-90% TG, lowest density, largest size, carry dietary/exogenous TG). VLDL: ~55% TG (carry endogenous TG from liver). IDL: intermediate. LDL: highest cholesterol content (~50% cholesterol). HDL: highest protein content (~50% protein, smallest, densest). Order of density: chylomicrons < VLDL < IDL < LDL < HDL. Order of TG content: chylomicrons > VLDL > IDL > LDL > HDL. Reference: Harper's — Lipoprotein Composition.

questions.questionText: Chylomicrons are the largest lipoproteins (>1000 nm) and give plasma a milky appearance. A patient with fasting lipemic plasma has:
questions.optionB: Chylomicrons should be cleared within 12 hours of eating; fasting lipemia indicates either LPL deficiency (type I hyperlipoproteinemia) or ApoC-II deficiency → failure to hydrolyze chylomicron TG; refrigerator test shows creamy supernatant layer
questions.explanation: Fasting lipemia — diagnostic approach: Normal chylomicron metabolism: dietary TG → chylomicrons (ApoB-48, ApoC-II, ApoE) → LPL (on capillary endothelium, activated by ApoC-II) → hydrolyzes TG → chylomicron remnants → cleared by liver (ApoE → LDL-related protein receptor). t½ <12 hours. FASTING lipemia (>12h fast): chylomicrons STILL present → clearance defect: (1) Familial LPL deficiency (type I): AR, severe hypertriglyceridemia (TG >1000 mg/dL), eruptive xanthomas, hepatosplenomegaly, lipemia retinalis, pancreatitis risk. (2) ApoC-II deficiency: same phenotype (LPL not activated). Refrigerator test: leave plasma at 4°C overnight → chylomicrons float → CREAMY layer on top (vs VLDL excess shows uniform turbidity). Type I: elevated chylomicrons only. Type V: both chylomicrons + VLDL elevated. Reference: Harper's — Chylomicron Clearance.
questions.conceptTag: Fasting lipemia chylomicron LPL ApoC-II deficiency

questions.questionText: Orlistat (anti-obesity drug) works by inhibiting:
questions.optionA: Pancreatic lipase (reduces dietary fat absorption by ~30%)
questions.explanation: Orlistat: inhibits gastric and pancreatic lipases → prevents hydrolysis of dietary triglycerides → unabsorbed fat excreted in feces (steatorrhea). Reduces fat absorption by ~30%. Side effects: oily stools, fecal urgency, flatulence with discharge, fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption (need supplementation). Also reduces cholesterol absorption. Indicated for obesity with BMI ≥ 30 (or ≥ 27 with comorbidities). Does not affect carbohydrate or protein absorption. Reference: Harper's — Lipase Inhibitors.

questions.questionText: Carnitine shuttle system is inhibited in the fed state by:
questions.optionD: Malonyl-CoA (product of ACC, inhibits CPT-I → prevents fatty acid oxidation when synthesis is active)
questions.explanation: Reciprocal regulation of fatty acid synthesis and oxidation: In fed state: insulin activates ACC → malonyl-CoA production increases → malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I (carnitine palmitoyltransferase I) → fatty acids CANNOT enter mitochondria → β-oxidation blocked → fatty acids directed toward esterification and storage. In fasting: glucagon activates AMPK → phosphorylates and inactivates ACC → malonyl-CoA decreases → CPT-I active → β-oxidation proceeds. This ensures synthesis and oxidation don't occur simultaneously (futile cycle prevention). Reference: Harper's — Malonyl-CoA CPT-I Regulation.

questions.questionText: Orlistat (anti-obesity drug) inhibits gastric and pancreatic lipases. The main clinical concern besides GI side effects is:
questions.optionA: FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMIN (A, D, E, K) MALABSORPTION — since lipase inhibition reduces TG digestion by ~30%, the fat-soluble vitamins dissolved in dietary fat are also malabsorbed; patients require vitamin supplementation taken 2 hours apart from orlistat
questions.explanation: Orlistat — mechanism and clinical concerns: Mechanism: irreversible inhibitor of gastric lipase and pancreatic lipase → blocks ~30% dietary TG hydrolysis → undigested fat excreted in stool. GI side effects: steatorrhea, oily spotting, fecal urgency (fat in colon → osmotic effects). Fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption: vitamins A, D, E, K are dissolved in dietary fat → require lipase digestion and micellar solubilization for absorption. With orlistat: reduced fat digestion → reduced micelle formation → DECREASED absorption of A, D, E, K. Recommendation: take multivitamin supplement AT LEAST 2 HOURS before or after orlistat dose (time separation ensures vitamin is absorbed before lipase is inhibited). Vitamin K deficiency risk: enhanced if on warfarin concurrently → monitor INR. Rare: oxalate nephropathy (unabsorbed fat binds calcium → free oxalate absorbed → kidney stones). Reference: Harper's — Orlistat.

questions.questionText: A patient with extremely elevated triglycerides (>1000 mg/dL), eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and recurrent pancreatitis most likely has which Fredrickson phenotype?
questions.optionB: Type I (elevated chylomicrons) or Type V (elevated chylomicrons + VLDL)
questions.explanation: Fredrickson classification of hyperlipidemias — clinical integration: Type I: familial LPL or ApoC-II deficiency → chylomicron accumulation → TG >1000, eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, pancreatitis, NO increased atherosclerosis risk (chylomicrons too large for arterial wall). Type IIa: familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) → LDL receptor defect → elevated LDL-cholesterol → tendon xanthomas, premature atherosclerosis. Type IIb: combined → LDL + VLDL elevated. Type III (dysbetalipoproteinemia): ApoE2/E2 homozygosity → IDL accumulation → palmar xanthomas (pathognomonic), tuberoeruptive xanthomas. Type IV: elevated VLDL only. Type V: both chylomicrons + VLDL → very high TG → pancreatitis risk. Reference: Harper's — Fredrickson Classification.

questions.questionText: PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) lower LDL cholesterol by:
questions.optionB: Preventing PCSK9-mediated lysosomal degradation of LDL receptors → more LDL receptors recycled to hepatocyte surface → increased LDL clearance
questions.optionC: Blocking cholesterol absorption in the intestine
questions.explanation: PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9) mechanism: normally, after LDL binds LDL receptor and is internalized, the receptor is recycled to the cell surface. PCSK9 (secreted by hepatocytes) binds to LDL receptor extracellularly → targets the receptor-PCSK9 complex for lysosomal degradation instead of recycling → fewer surface LDL receptors → less LDL clearance → higher LDL cholesterol. PCSK9 inhibitors (monoclonal antibodies): block PCSK9 → LDL receptors recycled and accumulate on hepatocyte surface → dramatically increase LDL clearance → LDL reduction of 50-70% (even on top of statins). Gain-of-function PCSK9 mutations → FH phenotype. Loss-of-function mutations → very low LDL, cardioprotective. Reference: Harper's — PCSK9 Pathway.

questions.questionText: Lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] is an independent cardiovascular risk factor because:
questions.optionA: Lp(a) is structurally similar to plasminogen → competitively inhibits fibrinolysis AND promotes atherogenesis by delivering cholesterol to arterial walls
questions.explanation: Lp(a): LDL-like particle with apolipoprotein(a) [apo(a)] covalently bound to ApoB-100 via disulfide bond. Apo(a) has structural homology to plasminogen (contains kringle domains) → competitively inhibits plasminogen binding to fibrin → impairs fibrinolysis → prothrombotic. Additionally: Lp(a) is atherogenic like LDL (deposits cholesterol in arterial wall), pro-inflammatory (carries oxidized phospholipids). Levels are largely GENETICALLY determined (LPA gene — kringle IV type 2 repeat number, inversely proportional to Lp(a) level). NOT significantly lowered by statins or lifestyle changes. Elevated Lp(a) > 50 mg/dL increases CVD risk 2-3x. Emerging therapies: antisense oligonucleotides (pelacarsen targeting LPA mRNA). Reference: Harper's — Lipoprotein(a).

questions.questionText: A boy presents with progressive spastic paraparesis, adrenal insufficiency, and elevated plasma very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFA). The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (X-ALD): most common peroxisomal disorder. ABCD1 gene mutation → defective peroxisomal membrane transporter (ABC transporter) → VLCFA (C26:0, C24:0) cannot enter peroxisomes for β-oxidation → accumulate in plasma, adrenal cortex, and CNS white matter. Forms: (1) Childhood cerebral ALD (most severe — progressive dementia, blindness, spastic quadriplegia, adrenal failure, death within years), (2) Adrenomyeloneuropathy (AMN — adult, slower, spinal cord involvement), (3) Addison-only (isolated adrenal insufficiency). Diagnosis: elevated plasma VLCFA (C26:0/C22:0 ratio). Treatment: Lorenzo's oil (4:1 oleic:erucic acid — normalizes VLCFA but doesn't reverse neurological damage), HSCT (if early cerebral disease). Differs from Zellweger (ALL peroxisomal functions lost). Reference: Harper's — X-ALD.

questions.questionText: The myocardium preferentially uses which fuel source under normal resting conditions?
questions.optionD: Fatty acids (60-70% of cardiac ATP), with the remainder from glucose, lactate, and ketone bodies
questions.explanation: Cardiac fuel preferences — organ-specific metabolism: Heart is the most metabolically active organ (highest O2 consumption per gram). Under normal resting conditions: fatty acids provide 60-70% of ATP (via β-oxidation), glucose ~20%, lactate ~10-15%, ketone bodies ~5%. Heart is an 'omnivore' — uses multiple fuel sources. During ISCHEMIA: O2 deprivation → FA oxidation impaired → switches to anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulates (heart becomes lactate PRODUCER instead of consumer). During DKA/starvation: ketone bodies become major cardiac fuel. During exercise: lactate from skeletal muscle used by heart (lactate is a fuel, not a waste product for the heart). Heart has very low glycogen stores → cannot rely on glycogenolysis. Reference: Harper's — Cardiac Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Tangier disease is characterized by extremely low HDL levels and orange tonsils due to deficiency of:
questions.optionD: ABCA1 transporter (ATP-binding cassette transporter A1 — required for cholesterol efflux from cells to nascent HDL)
questions.explanation: Tangier disease: autosomal recessive ABCA1 transporter deficiency → impaired reverse cholesterol transport. ABCA1 normally mediates efflux of cholesterol and phospholipids from peripheral cells to lipid-poor ApoA-I → forms nascent HDL (discoidal). Without ABCA1: cholesterol cannot be exported → accumulates in macrophages of tonsils (orange-yellow enlarged tonsils — pathognomonic), liver, spleen, lymph nodes, cornea. Lab: extremely low HDL (<5 mg/dL), low ApoA-I, mild hypertriglyceridemia. Moderate increase in atherosclerosis risk despite very low HDL. LCAT deficiency (fish-eye disease): also low HDL but with corneal opacities and kidney disease. CETP deficiency: elevated HDL (opposite). Reference: Harper's — Tangier Disease ABCA1.

questions.questionText: Familial hypercholesterolemia heterozygotes (one mutant LDL receptor allele) typically have LDL cholesterol levels of approximately:
questions.optionC: 300-400 mg/dL (about 2x normal) with premature atherosclerosis and tendon xanthomas
questions.explanation: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) — gene dosage effect: FH is autosomal DOMINANT. LDL receptor mutations → impaired LDL clearance. Heterozygous FH (1 in 250-500): ~50% functional LDL receptors → LDL cholesterol ~300-400 mg/dL (2x normal) → tendon xanthomas (Achilles, knuckles), xanthelasma, corneal arcus, premature CAD (MI by age 40-55). Homozygous FH (1 in million): ~0-25% receptor activity → LDL >600-1000 mg/dL → MI in childhood/adolescence, planar xanthomas. Treatment: statins (first-line for heterozygotes), PCSK9 inhibitors, ezetimibe, LDL apheresis (for homozygotes), liver transplant (homozygotes — provides normal LDL receptors). FH is due to mutations in: LDLR (most common), ApoB-100, PCSK9 (gain-of-function). Reference: Harper's — FH Gene Dosage.
questions.conceptTag: FH heterozygous vs homozygous gene dosage

questions.questionText: A neonate presents with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and elevated plasma acylcarnitines (octanoylcarnitine peak). The enzyme deficiency is most likely:
questions.explanation: MCAD deficiency: most common inherited fatty acid oxidation disorder (1:10,000-15,000). MCAD catalyzes the first step of β-oxidation for C6-C12 fatty acyl-CoAs. Deficiency → medium-chain FAs cannot be oxidized → accumulate as acylcarnitines (C8/octanoylcarnitine is the characteristic biomarker on newborn screening). Clinical: hypoketotic hypoglycemia (hepatic ketogenesis impaired — no acetyl-CoA production from medium-chain FAs), lethargy, vomiting, hepatomegaly, Reye-like episodes. Triggered by: fasting, illness (increased FA dependence). Fatal without treatment. Key: HYPOketotic distinguishes FA oxidation defects from ketotic hypoglycemia. Management: avoid fasting, frequent feeds, IV glucose during illness. Included in newborn screening (tandem mass spectrometry). Reference: Harper's — MCAD Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Statins can cause myopathy and rarely rhabdomyolysis because inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase:
questions.optionA: Increases uric acid levels causing crystal deposition in muscles
questions.optionB: Blocks fatty acid oxidation in muscle
questions.optionC: Directly damages sarcolemma through cholesterol depletion only
questions.optionD: Depletes isoprenoid intermediates (geranylgeranyl-PP, farnesyl-PP) needed for protein prenylation and CoQ10 synthesis → impaired skeletal muscle mitochondrial function
questions.explanation: Statin myopathy biochemistry: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting step of the mevalonate pathway. Statins block not just cholesterol synthesis but ALL products of this pathway including: (1) Isoprenoids (geranylgeranyl-PP, farnesyl-PP): essential for protein PRENYLATION (post-translational lipid modification → membrane anchoring and signaling of small GTPases like Ras, Rho). Depletion → impaired muscle cell signaling and apoptosis. (2) Coenzyme Q10 (ubiquinone): electron carrier in ETC. Depletion → impaired mitochondrial respiration → oxidative stress → muscle damage. (3) Dolichol: needed for glycoprotein synthesis. Risk factors for statin myopathy: high dose, CYP3A4 inhibitors (erythromycin, ketoconazole — increase statin blood levels), hypothyroidism, renal impairment, combination with fibrates/niacin, genetic variants (SLCO1B1). Reference: Harper's — Statin Myopathy Mechanism.

questions.questionText: Cholestasis (bile duct obstruction) causes malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins. The EARLIEST vitamin to become deficient is:
questions.optionB: Vitamin K (shortest body stores, 1-2 weeks → prolonged PT/INR is earliest sign)
questions.explanation: Fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K) malabsorption in cholestasis: bile salts are required for micelle formation → fat and fat-soluble vitamin absorption. In bile duct obstruction → no bile salts reach intestine → steatorrhea and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency. Order of deficiency based on body stores: Vitamin K (shortest stores, 1-2 weeks) → EARLIEST to become deficient → prolonged PT/INR (earliest lab sign of fat malabsorption, used clinically). Vitamin A (weeks-months). Vitamin D (months — liver stores 25-OH-D). Vitamin E (longest stores, months-years). Clinical significance: prolonged PT that corrects with parenteral vitamin K (but NOT oral — absorption impaired) = obstructive jaundice. If PT doesn't correct → hepatocellular disease (liver cannot synthesize clotting factors). Reference: Harper's — Fat-Soluble Vitamin Malabsorption.
questions.conceptTag: Cholestasis fat-soluble vitamin K earliest deficiency

questions.questionText: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA) reduce cardiovascular risk primarily through which biochemical mechanism?
questions.optionB: Reducing hepatic VLDL synthesis and secretion → lowering triglycerides; also producing anti-inflammatory eicosanoids (resolvins, protectins)
questions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA — C20:5ω3, DHA — C22:6ω3): mechanisms of cardiovascular benefit: (1) Reduced hepatic VLDL synthesis: EPA/DHA suppress SREBP-1c (transcription factor for lipogenesis) and activate PPARα (increases β-oxidation) → fewer triglycerides packaged into VLDL → lower plasma TG (25-50% reduction at pharmacological doses). (2) Anti-inflammatory: EPA competes with arachidonic acid (AA/ω-6) for COX/LOX → produces less potent inflammatory mediators (PGI3, TXA3, LTB5 vs potent PGI2, TXA2, LTB4). Additionally: resolvins (from EPA) and protectins/maresins (from DHA) actively RESOLVE inflammation. (3) Antiarrhythmic: stabilize cardiac myocyte membranes. (4) Antiplatelet: reduce TXA2. Sources: fatty fish, fish oil, flaxseed (ALA, a precursor). Reference: Harper's — Omega-3 Mechanisms.
questions.conceptTag: Omega-3 EPA DHA anti-inflammatory TG lowering

questions.questionText: CETP (cholesteryl ester transfer protein) facilitates the transfer of:
questions.optionA: Cholesteryl esters from HDL to VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides
questions.optionB: Phospholipids from cell membranes to lipoproteins
questions.optionD: Free fatty acids between albumin and lipoproteins
questions.explanation: CETP: plasma protein that mediates heteroexchange of neutral lipids between lipoproteins. Transfers cholesteryl esters (CE) from HDL → VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides (TG) from VLDL/LDL → HDL. Physiological role: part of reverse cholesterol transport (indirect pathway). Clinical significance: (1) CETP deficiency (genetic): very HIGH HDL (hyperalphalipoproteinemia) — common in Japanese populations. Whether this is protective is debated. (2) CETP inhibitors (torcetrapib, anacetrapib, dalcetrapib): pharmaceutical attempts to raise HDL by blocking CETP. Torcetrapib failed (increased mortality — off-target effects on aldosterone). Anacetrapib showed modest CVD benefit. This taught us: raising HDL number alone doesn't guarantee protection — HDL FUNCTION matters. Reference: Harper's — CETP and HDL Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Ezetimibe lowers LDL cholesterol by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: NPC1L1 (Niemann-Pick C1-Like 1 protein) — the intestinal cholesterol absorption transporter at the brush border of enterocytes
questions.optionC: Bile acid reabsorption in the ileum
questions.explanation: Ezetimibe mechanism: selectively inhibits NPC1L1 (Niemann-Pick C1-Like 1) transporter on the brush border of jejunal enterocytes. NPC1L1 mediates the critical step of intestinal cholesterol absorption (both dietary and biliary cholesterol). Inhibition → reduced cholesterol absorption (~50%) → less chylomicron cholesterol delivery to liver → liver upregulates LDL receptors (compensatory) → increased LDL clearance from blood → LDL reduction ~15-20%. Often combined with statins for additive effect (statin reduces synthesis + ezetimibe reduces absorption = dual mechanism). Ezetimibe does NOT affect triglyceride or fat-soluble vitamin absorption. Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine): different mechanism — bind bile acids in gut. Orlistat: inhibits pancreatic lipase (reduces fat absorption, not specifically cholesterol). Reference: Harper's — Ezetimibe NPC1L1.

questions.questionText: Fibrates (fenofibrate, gemfibrozil) primarily lower triglycerides by activating:
questions.optionD: PPARα (peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha) → increases LPL expression, β-oxidation genes, and ApoA-I while decreasing ApoC-III
questions.explanation: Fibrate mechanism — detailed biochemistry: Fibrates are PPARα agonists. PPARα is a nuclear receptor transcription factor predominantly expressed in liver, heart, muscle, and kidney. Activation → (1) Increased LPL expression → enhanced TG-rich lipoprotein clearance, (2) Decreased ApoC-III synthesis (ApoC-III normally inhibits LPL → less inhibition → more lipolysis), (3) Increased fatty acid β-oxidation genes → reduced hepatic TG, (4) Increased ApoA-I and ApoA-II → raised HDL. Net effect: TG reduction 30-50%, HDL increase 10-20%, modest LDL changes. Clinical use: hypertriglyceridemia (prevents pancreatitis), mixed dyslipidemia. Drug interaction: gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation → increased statin myopathy risk (prefer fenofibrate with statins). Reference: Harper's — Fibrate PPARα Mechanism.

questions.questionText: A 10-year-old child presents with angiokeratomas, decreased sweating, and burning pain in his extremities. His grandfather died of cardiovascular complications and renal failure. The accumulated lipid in this patient's tissues is:
questions.explanation: The patient has Fabry disease, an X-linked recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by a deficiency of alpha-galactosidase A. This leads to the accumulation of ceramide trihexoside (globotriaosylceramide). Clinical features include angiokeratomas, hypohidrosis, acroparesthesias, and late-stage renal and cardiovascular failure.

questions.questionText: Complete mitochondrial oxidation of palmitate (C16:0) yields net ATP closest to:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: 106 ATP using modern P/O values. Palmitate gives 7 beta-oxidation cycles (7 NADH, 7 FADH2) and 8 acetyl-CoA; TCA from 8 acetyl-CoA yields 24 NADH, 8 FADH2, 8 GTP. Total ATP equivalent is 108, minus 2 ATP for activation = 106. Others are wrong because they use outdated assumptions or incorrect cycle accounting. Key concept: fat is highly reduced and energy-dense.

questions.questionText: Compared with complete oxidation of one glucose molecule, complete oxidation of one palmitate molecule yields more ATP primarily because palmitate:
questions.optionB: Produces ATP directly without reducing equivalents
questions.optionC: Is more reduced, generating a larger NADH/FADH2 load per molecule
questions.optionD: Consumes less oxygen per carbon oxidized
questions.explanation: Correct answer: fatty acids are more reduced than carbohydrates, so oxidation generates more electron carriers and more ATP. A is wrong because acetyl-CoA from fatty acids enters TCA. B is wrong because oxidative phosphorylation still depends on reducing equivalents. D is wrong because fat has lower respiratory quotient and requires relatively more oxygen per carbon. Clinical relevance: explains high caloric density of fat.
questions.conceptTag: Fat vs glucose reducing equivalent comparison

questions.questionText: Which respiratory quotient (RQ) pairing is correct in mixed-fuel physiology?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: carbohydrate oxidation has RQ about 1.0, while fat oxidation has RQ about 0.7. Others are incorrect swaps or non-standard values. Mechanistic clue: fats consume proportionally more oxygen per carbon oxidized. Clinical significance: indirect calorimetry uses RQ to infer substrate use.

questions.questionText: A 4-month-old infant develops seizures after overnight fasting. Labs: glucose low, ketones low, ammonia mildly high, liver enlarged. Acylcarnitine profile shows low long-chain acylcarnitines with high free carnitine. Most likely defect:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: CPT-I deficiency (hepatic-predominant) blocks long-chain acylcarnitine formation at outer mitochondrial membrane, causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia. A usually shows elevated medium-chain acylcarnitines (especially C8). B typically has very low plasma carnitine and often cardiomyopathy. C is more myopathic with exercise/rhabdomyolysis phenotype. Key trap: low long-chain acylcarnitines points to CPT-I.

questions.questionText: A 19-year-old man has recurrent myalgia and myoglobinuria after prolonged exercise and fasting. Between attacks he is well. Which diagnosis is most likely?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: CPT-II deficiency classically causes exercise-induced muscle symptoms and rhabdomyolysis. A usually presents with systemic low carnitine and cardiomyopathy. C presents earlier with fasting hypoketotic hypoglycemia and liver-predominant involvement. D gives triglyceride-pancreatitis phenotype. Differentiating clue: exertional myoglobinuria strongly suggests muscle FA-oxidation entry defect.

questions.questionText: A child has cardiomyopathy, muscle weakness, and recurrent hypoketotic hypoglycemia. Plasma free carnitine is markedly low. Which defect best explains this profile?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: OCTN2 defect causes systemic primary carnitine deficiency with low plasma/tissue carnitine, impaired long-chain FA transport, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, and cardiomyopathy. B usually has preserved/high free carnitine with low long-chain acylcarnitines. C has medium-chain pattern (C8). D mainly affects VLCFA handling and does not fit this carnitine profile. Clinical relevance: carnitine supplementation can be life-saving.

questions.questionText: Which acylcarnitine profile most strongly supports MCAD deficiency over primary carnitine deficiency?
questions.optionA: Markedly reduced total carnitine with globally low acylcarnitines
questions.optionB: Predominant increase in long-chain acylcarnitines C16-C18
questions.optionD: Normal acylcarnitine profile with isolated high LDL
questions.explanation: Correct answer: MCAD deficiency shows a characteristic medium-chain acylcarnitine rise, especially C8. A suggests primary carnitine transporter deficiency. B suggests long-chain transport/oxidation defects such as CPT-II or VLCAD patterns. D is unrelated to FA oxidation disorders. Key exam clue: C8 spike plus fasting hypoketotic episodes.

questions.questionText: In alcoholic ketoacidosis, beta-hydroxybutyrate to acetoacetate ratio is increased mainly because:
questions.optionA: CPT-I is strongly activated by insulin
questions.optionB: LCAT depletion diverts acetoacetate to HDL
questions.optionC: High hepatic NADH/NAD+ drives acetoacetate reduction to beta-hydroxybutyrate
questions.explanation: Correct answer: ethanol metabolism raises NADH, shifting acetoacetate toward beta-hydroxybutyrate. A is opposite hormonal logic. B is unrelated lipoprotein chemistry. D is false because RBCs do not run ketolysis. Clinical relevance: nitroprusside urine ketone tests under-detect beta-hydroxybutyrate-dominant states.

questions.questionText: Why can erythrocytes neither oxidize fatty acids nor utilize ketone bodies for ATP generation?
questions.optionC: They lack both ApoE and ApoA-I
questions.optionD: They lack mitochondria, so beta-oxidation, TCA cycle, and ketolysis cannot occur
questions.explanation: Correct answer: RBCs are mitochondria-free and rely on glycolysis and PPP only. A and B are incorrect because RBC glycolysis requires NAD+ regeneration via lactate dehydrogenase and glucose transport is present. C concerns lipoprotein particles, not RBC ATP pathways. Key trap: RBC cannot use ketones even during starvation.
questions.conceptTag: RBC inability to use FA and ketones

questions.questionText: Which profile best distinguishes DKA, starvation ketosis, and alcoholic ketoacidosis?
questions.optionA: Starvation ketosis has highest glucose and severe dehydration
questions.optionB: DKA usually has marked hyperglycemia and high anion gap acidosis; starvation ketosis has milder acidosis with low-normal glucose; alcoholic ketoacidosis often has high beta-hydroxybutyrate with normal/low glucose
questions.optionC: Alcoholic ketoacidosis requires persistent insulin excess
questions.explanation: Correct answer: this is the key high-yield comparison. A is false because starvation ketosis is generally milder with lower glucose. C is false because alcoholic ketoacidosis occurs with low intake, stress hormones, and altered redox state. D is false because glucose, hydration, and ketone species distribution differ. Clinical relevance: management differs significantly.

questions.questionText: For synthesis of one molecule of palmitate (C16:0) from acetyl-CoA, the required high-yield stoichiometry is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: one acetyl-CoA is primer and 7 malonyl-CoA units are generated using 7 ATP by ACC, then reduction steps consume 14 NADPH. B and D swap ATP logic. C incorrectly replaces NADPH with NADH and carbon accounting is wrong. Key mechanism: cytosolic fatty acid synthase depends on NADPH, not NADH.

questions.questionText: Which statement correctly contrasts ACP and CoA in lipid metabolism?
questions.optionA: ACP is a circulating plasma apoprotein
questions.optionB: CoA is a fixed domain of fatty acid synthase
questions.optionC: ACP carries growing acyl chains on fatty acid synthase, while CoA carries acyl groups in catabolic and transport reactions
questions.optionD: Both are functionally interchangeable without enzyme specificity
questions.explanation: Correct answer: ACP is the phosphopantetheine-containing carrier domain within fatty acid synthase complex, whereas CoA is a diffusible acyl carrier in many pathways including beta-oxidation. A and B are false identity swaps. D is false because enzyme active sites are specific for ACP- or CoA-linked substrates. Trap: both use pantetheine chemistry but not interchangeable biologic roles.

questions.questionText: When cellular sterol is low, SREBP-dependent cholesterol regulation proceeds via:
questions.optionA: Direct nuclear translocation of LDL receptor protein
questions.optionB: Immediate ACAT activation with SREBP retention in ER
questions.optionD: SCAP escorts SREBP from ER to Golgi for proteolytic activation and nuclear entry
questions.explanation: Correct answer: low sterol releases SCAP-Insig retention, allowing SREBP processing in Golgi and transcriptional upregulation of LDLR and HMG-CoA reductase genes. A is not how regulation occurs. B describes high-sterol storage behavior. C relates to bile acid signaling, not primary SREBP-SCAP control. Clinical significance: explains statin-induced LDLR upregulation.

questions.questionText: In hepatocytes, insulin versus glucagon regulation of ACC is best described by:
questions.optionA: Insulin activates AMPK to phosphorylate and inhibit ACC
questions.optionB: Glucagon promotes ACC dephosphorylation and lipogenesis
questions.optionC: Both hormones activate ACC equally through cAMP
questions.optionD: Insulin favors ACC dephosphorylation (active), whereas glucagon/AMPK-mediated phosphorylation inhibits ACC
questions.explanation: Correct answer: insulin drives dephosphorylation and lipogenesis; glucagon and low-energy AMPK signaling phosphorylate and inhibit ACC. A and B are reversed physiology. C is false because hormonal signals are reciprocal, not equivalent. Key trap: phosphorylation state has opposite effects on ACC versus glycogen phosphorylase.

questions.questionText: All are true regarding ACAT and LCAT EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: ACAT esterifies intracellular cholesterol for storage
questions.optionB: LCAT esterifies cholesterol in plasma on HDL particles
questions.optionD: ACAT is the principal plasma enzyme for HDL maturation
questions.explanation: Correct answer (EXCEPT): ACAT is intracellular, not the plasma HDL-maturation enzyme. A, B, and C are true. Key trap: ACAT and LCAT names sound similar but compartments and physiologic roles differ. Clinical relevance: LCAT defects impair HDL maturation, while ACAT pertains to intracellular cholesterol ester storage.

questions.questionText: Bile acid feedback suppression of cholesterol 7 alpha-hydroxylase (CYP7A1) is mediated primarily by:
questions.optionA: FXR signaling in liver and intestine with downstream repression of CYP7A1 expression
questions.explanation: Correct answer: bile acids activate FXR, which triggers pathways (including SHP and intestinal FGF19 axis) that repress CYP7A1 and limit further bile acid synthesis. B is opposite to cholesterol-lowering state signaling. C and D are unrelated to this feedback loop. Clinical significance: integrates enterohepatic circulation with hepatic cholesterol disposal.

questions.questionText: A gain-of-function PCSK9 variant raises LDL-C mainly because PCSK9:
questions.optionD: Targets LDL receptors for lysosomal degradation, reducing receptor recycling
questions.explanation: Correct answer: PCSK9 promotes LDLR degradation and reduces hepatic LDL clearance. A is statin mechanism, B relates to MTP/apoB48 pathway, C concerns HDL-VLDL neutral lipid exchange. Clinical relevance: PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL by preserving LDLR recycling.

questions.questionText: In the exogenous lipoprotein pathway after a fatty meal, which sequence is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Intestinal chylomicrons acquire ApoC-II and ApoE from HDL, lose triglyceride via LPL, and remnants are taken up by liver through ApoE-dependent receptors
questions.optionB: Chylomicrons convert directly to LDL in plasma
questions.optionC: ApoB-100 is required for chylomicron assembly in enterocytes
questions.optionD: Chylomicron remnants are cleared independent of ApoE
questions.explanation: Correct answer: this is the canonical exogenous pathway. B is wrong because LDL comes from VLDL/IDL endogenous cascade. C is wrong because chylomicrons use ApoB-48, not ApoB-100. D is wrong because ApoE-mediated remnant uptake is central. High-yield trap: ApoB-48 versus ApoB-100 confusion.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes the endogenous pathway from VLDL to LDL?
questions.optionA: VLDL is secreted from intestine and directly enters HDL pool
questions.optionB: LPL hydrolyzes VLDL triglyceride to form IDL; hepatic lipase further remodels IDL to LDL enriched in cholesteryl esters
questions.optionC: IDL cannot be taken up by liver
questions.optionD: LDL contains ApoB-48 as its structural apoprotein
questions.explanation: Correct answer: endogenous lipoprotein flux is VLDL to IDL to LDL, with LPL and hepatic lipase playing distinct roles. A and D are apoprotein/source errors. C is wrong because IDL can be removed by liver via ApoE pathways. Clinical significance: central to atherogenic LDL generation.
questions.conceptTag: Endogenous pathway VLDL IDL LDL integration

questions.questionText: Direct reverse cholesterol transport to liver is primarily mediated by:
questions.optionC: SR-B1 selective uptake of HDL cholesteryl esters
questions.explanation: Correct answer: SR-B1 enables selective uptake of HDL cholesteryl esters by liver without whole-particle endocytosis. A and B are incorrect receptor-ligand pairings. D is cholesterol absorption from gut, not reverse transport. Key pathway: HDL maturation plus hepatic selective uptake is a core anti-atherogenic axis.

questions.questionText: All of the following are true about hepatic lipase versus lipoprotein lipase EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: LPL is capillary endothelial and ApoC-II dependent
questions.optionB: Hepatic lipase contributes to IDL-to-LDL and HDL remodeling
questions.optionC: Hepatic lipase requires ApoC-II in the same way as LPL
questions.optionD: LPL mainly hydrolyzes triglycerides in chylomicrons and VLDL
questions.explanation: Correct answer (EXCEPT): hepatic lipase is not classically ApoC-II dependent like LPL. A, B, and D are true. Trap: both are lipases but location, substrate preference, and cofactor dependence differ. Clinical significance: explains distinct effects on postprandial triglycerides versus remnant/HDL remodeling.

questions.questionText: A patient has recurrent pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, and fasting triglycerides above 1500 mg/dL. Which risk statement is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Very high triglycerides markedly increase pancreatitis risk, while atherosclerotic risk pattern depends on the specific lipoprotein abnormality
questions.optionB: Pancreatitis risk is determined mainly by LDL concentration
questions.optionC: Triglycerides are irrelevant once HDL is normal
questions.optionD: Type IIa pattern is the classic severe chylomicronemia syndrome
questions.explanation: Correct answer: severe hypertriglyceridemia drives pancreatitis risk, especially with chylomicron-rich states. B and C are incorrect simplifications. D is wrong because Type IIa is LDL-predominant hypercholesterolemia, not classic chylomicronemia. Clinical integration: risk stratification differs for pancreatitis versus atherosclerosis.

questions.questionText: Foam cell formation in early atherogenesis is driven primarily by:
questions.optionA: LDLR-mediated uptake of native LDL with strict feedback downregulation
questions.optionB: FXR-mediated bile acid signaling in intima
questions.optionD: Macrophage scavenger-receptor uptake of oxidized LDL that is relatively unregulated by intracellular cholesterol
questions.explanation: Correct answer: oxidized LDL uptake via scavenger receptors promotes uncontrolled cholesterol loading and foam cell formation. A is regulated and less foam-cell driving. B is unrelated arterial wall mechanism. C is protective efflux, not foam-cell generation. Clinical relevance: links oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, and plaque initiation.
questions.conceptTag: Oxidized LDL scavenger receptor foam cell

questions.questionText: In mixed dyslipidemia requiring statin plus fibrate therapy, which choice is generally preferred to reduce severe myopathy risk?
questions.optionB: Fenofibrate with an appropriate-dose statin under monitoring
questions.optionC: Gemfibrozil with any statin because interaction is negligible
questions.optionD: Niacin plus statin is always safer than fibrate combinations
questions.explanation: Correct answer: fenofibrate is generally preferred with statins because gemfibrozil more strongly increases statin exposure and myopathy risk. A and C are unsafe assumptions. D is false as niacin combinations can also cause adverse effects. Pharmacology integration: mechanism-level interaction informs safer combination therapy.

questions.questionText: Niacin lowers triglycerides primarily by which mechanism?
questions.optionA: Direct inhibition of NPC1L1-mediated cholesterol absorption
questions.optionB: Decreasing adipose lipolysis and hepatic VLDL synthesis, with secondary reduction in LDL
questions.optionC: Upregulating PCSK9 to accelerate LDL receptor turnover
questions.explanation: Correct answer: niacin reduces FFA flux from adipose tissue to liver, lowering hepatic TG synthesis and VLDL output. A is ezetimibe mechanism. C is opposite of LDL-lowering biology. D is statin mechanism. Clinical relevance: niacin adverse effects (flushing, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia) often limit use.

questions.questionText: Bile acid sequestrants are most likely to worsen which baseline lipid abnormality?
questions.optionB: Isolated low HDL with normal triglycerides
questions.optionC: Low LDL due to overactive LDL receptor
questions.explanation: Correct answer: bile acid sequestrants can increase hepatic VLDL production and raise triglycerides, so marked hypertriglyceridemia is a key caution. B and D are not primary contraindication logic for this class. C is not a real use-case risk statement. Mechanism integration: reduced bile acid reabsorption drives cholesterol-to-bile acid conversion but may secondarily elevate TG.
questions.conceptTag: Bile acid sequestrant triglyceride rise trap

questions.questionText: An electrophoresis report shows broad beta band, and exam notes palmar xanthomas with mixed cholesterol and triglyceride elevation. Most likely diagnosis is:
questions.optionC: Type III dysbetalipoproteinemia (ApoE2/E2 remnant clearance defect)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Type III has broad beta pattern and palmar/tuberous xanthomas due to remnant accumulation, classically with ApoE2/E2 background. A is LDL-predominant without broad beta. B is chylomicronemia with eruptive xanthomas and pancreatitis pattern. D is VLDL-driven hypertriglyceridemia. High-yield trap: palmar xanthoma strongly points to Type III.
questions.conceptTag: Type III broad beta palmar xanthoma

questions.questionText: In beta-oxidation defects, urinary dicarboxylic aciduria occurs primarily because:
questions.optionA: Peroxisomal alpha-oxidation is overactive in all tissues
questions.optionB: CPT-I directly converts long-chain fatty acids into dicarboxylic acids
questions.optionC: Ketone body overproduction drains all urinary organic acids
questions.optionD: Blocked mitochondrial beta-oxidation diverts fatty acids to omega-oxidation in ER, producing dicarboxylic acids
questions.explanation: Correct answer: when mitochondrial beta-oxidation is impaired, omega-oxidation becomes a compensatory route and generates dicarboxylic acids detectable in urine. A and B are mechanistically false. C is opposite of hypoketotic states seen in many FA-oxidation defects. Clinical significance: dicarboxylic aciduria is a key diagnostic clue in FA oxidation disorders.

questions.questionText: During prolonged fasting, which organ-fuel pairing is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Heart mainly uses fatty acids, while brain shifts progressively to ketone bodies
questions.optionB: Brain mainly uses long-chain fatty acids crossing the blood-brain barrier
questions.optionC: Heart cannot oxidize ketone bodies under any condition
questions.optionD: Liver uses ketone bodies as its principal fuel due to high thiophorase activity
questions.explanation: Correct answer: heart is a major fatty-acid oxidizer and can also use ketones; brain progressively adapts to ketones in prolonged fasting. B is false because long-chain fatty acids do not significantly fuel brain. C is false because heart can use ketones. D is false because liver lacks thiophorase and cannot utilize ketones it produces.
questions.conceptTag: Organ-specific fuel switching heart brain fasting

questions.questionText: A patient on long-term valproate develops low plasma carnitine with worsening hyperammonemia and lethargy. This best represents:
questions.optionB: CPT-II deficiency with isolated skeletal muscle involvement
questions.optionC: Secondary carnitine deficiency with impaired fatty acid transport and ammonia handling stress
questions.optionD: MCAD deficiency due to ACADM mutation
questions.explanation: Correct answer: valproate can deplete carnitine secondarily, impairing mitochondrial fatty acid handling and aggravating metabolic stress including hyperammonemia risk. A and D are primary genetic disorders. B is a different inherited shuttle defect. Clinical relevance: this is a pharmacology-metabolism crossover where carnitine supplementation may be considered.

questions.questionText: Which comparison most reliably distinguishes a fasting beta-oxidation defect from a glycolytic myopathy such as McArdle disease?
questions.optionA: FA-oxidation defects classically cause fasting hypoketotic hypoglycemia, whereas McArdle causes exercise-induced muscle symptoms with absent lactate rise on forearm testing
questions.optionB: Both primarily present with isolated postprandial hyperglycemia and low CK
questions.optionC: McArdle disease is marked by severe neonatal hypoketotic hypoglycemia
questions.optionD: Beta-oxidation defects consistently show normal acylcarnitine profiles
questions.explanation: Correct answer: this is the key clinical separation. FA-oxidation defects are fasting-intolerance disorders with hypoketotic hypoglycemia; McArdle is an exercise-intolerance glycogenolysis defect with characteristic lactate non-rise and muscle findings. B, C, and D are incorrect pattern statements. High-yield integration: timing trigger (fasting vs exertion) is diagnostic.
questions.conceptTag: FA oxidation defect versus glycolytic myopathy comparison

questions.questionText: A patient has premature ASCVD despite normal LDL-C and HDL-C. Which lipoprotein abnormality is an important residual-risk explanation?
questions.optionC: Elevated lipoprotein(a), which is proatherogenic and prothrombotic
questions.optionD: Complete CETP deficiency with very high HDL
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Lp(a) is an LDL-like particle with apo(a) that contributes to residual cardiovascular risk independent of routine LDL/HDL levels. A and B are not risk explanations in this context. D does not directly explain this classic residual-risk pattern. Exam trap: normal standard lipid panel does not exclude high Lp(a)-mediated risk.
